artifacts.content:
From: daniel.t.nguyen.hr@gmail.com
To: julie.nguyen@csus.edu
Subject: Checking in on you + all this HR training I keep doing

Hey Julie,

I was thinking about you on the drive home from Elk Grove tonight and realized how much of my life the last few years has quietly revolved around our family’s health stuff.

Remember fall 2023 when you were at Sutter Medical Center in Sacramento after that depressive episode? I still think about visiting you on 5 North after work and then logging into that 6‑week Job Accommodation Network course in my apartment, trying to absorb everything about FMLA, ADA, and mental‑health accommodations. I’ve still got all the slides and case law summaries sitting in a Google Drive folder tied to this Gmail. Half of it was for work, but honestly half was me trying to be ready in case you ever need help navigating leave at a hospital job.

Same thing back in Jan–Apr 2022 with Mom. I signed up for that 10‑week evening course at Kaiser Roseville’s training center about managing chronic conditions and intermittent FMLA/CFRA because she’d missed those 6 weeks at the Elk Grove nail salon with the diabetes/neuropathy flare. Every case study sounded like her boss.

Then there’s Dad’s stuff, Mom’s stroke, now her chemo at Kaiser South Sac, and your medication error at UC Davis Med Center last spring that pushed Miguel and me to build that just‑culture coaching template for our Midtown clinic.

Sometimes it feels like my whole HR “specialization” is just our family’s medical chart turned into policy language. I don’t mind it, but I do worry about you carrying all this on top of nursing school.

How are you really doing this week? Want to grab phở at Uncle Thomas’s on Stockton this weekend and just vent?

Love,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Checking in on you + all this HR training I keep doing

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.t.nguyen.hr@gmail.com
To: thomas.pham.saigongarden@gmail.com
Subject: HR cleanup plan for Saigon Garden / Lotus / Saigon Corner

Chú Thomas,

I finally sat down and mapped out everything we’ve been doing with your restaurants. It’s a lot when you look at it together, but it also means we’re in a way better place than before.

First was August 2023 at the Stockton Blvd spot, when we caught those two servers clocking in their friends. Setting up the simple onboarding checklist, the cash‑tip reporting log, and that corrective action form is what helped you recover about $1,200 in overpaid wages over six weeks. That’s textbook wage‑and‑hour compliance now.

Then in March 2024 I did that weekend certificate workshop at UC Davis School of Law on harassment and retaliation claims involving undocumented employees. I kept thinking about your former dishwasher “Linh” and the quiet wage‑theft/harassment settlement from Saigon Garden in South Sacramento. The training on U‑visas and how not to expose immigration status in files is exactly why I keep nagging you about what goes in your personnel binders.

On top of that, we’ve got the IRS audit from July 2023 hanging over Saigon Garden’s payroll and tips, plus the back‑rent scare at Saigon Corner last October. No wonder I’m spending half my Fridays 6–8 p.m. with your tip sheets and half my Sundays 11–3 on the floor.

I don’t mind helping; I just want you to get to a point where the systems run without me. Let’s block one night next week where I come by Saigon Garden, we pull everything—timecards, tip logs, the new corrective action forms—and build a single checklist you can follow.

Love,
Daniel

metadata.subject: HR cleanup plan for Saigon Garden / Lotus / Saigon Corner

artifacts.content:
Title: Work/Family/Health – why my schedule is so rigid

Lately I keep feeling guilty when managers side‑eye my “no weekends, limited evenings” boundaries, so I need a clear picture for myself.

Mondays: I already told Mom I’ll keep taking her to Kaiser South Sacramento for the 7:00 p.m. PT sessions after her mild stroke in March 2023, so I really do have to be out by 6. On top of that, the first Monday of each month since Feb 2024 is Dad’s PTSD group at the Sacramento VA Medical Center; I leave work at 4, drive him, then usually log back in from the apartment for another hour.

Tuesdays: Trying to keep 5–7 p.m. clean because of that 8‑week CBT group for anxiety/burnout at Sacramento Community Mental Health Center that started June 2024. That’s directly tied to how fried I got during open enrollment and the panic attacks.

Wednesdays: Historically my late day for Riverbend metrics, but now it’s also when I meet Uncle Thomas at Pham’s Saigon Kitchen in South Sacramento to go over payroll and overtime issues from the June 2024 wage complaint. Hard to say no when I’m the one who helped him after the Labor Commissioner and IRS both started poking around.

Thursdays: Between Mom’s dialysis runs to Kaiser South Sac on 2nd/4th Thursdays and her newer chemo schedule plus my monthly telehealth with Dr. Harper at Sutter Psych, there’s not much flex.

Weekends: Saturdays are either Mom’s chemo or my HR burnout group at Sacramento Community Wellness Center; Sundays are St. Mary’s shelter or Saigon Corner lunch shift.

No wonder 8–5:30 M–F feels like the only way I can stay sane.

metadata.title: Work/Family/Health – why my schedule is so rigid

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.t.nguyen.hr@gmail.com
To: emily.tran.teach@gmail.com
Subject: Why I keep signing up for every heavy HR training

Hey Em,

You joked the other night that half my free time is just “sad HR webinars,” and you’re not totally wrong. I’ve been trying to untangle why I keep gravitating to the intense stuff.

It kind of started with Mom. After her diabetes/neuropathy flare in late 2021 and those 6 weeks she missed at the Elk Grove salon, I took that 10‑week evening course at Kaiser Roseville in early 2022 on chronic conditions and intermittent FMLA/CFRA. Every scenario sounded like her boss mishandling leave.

Then when your mom was at Mercy General in East Sac and you were on that reduced schedule from July–October 2022, I was buried in CFRA/FMLA paperwork at Riverbend, chasing Dr. Patel’s certifications and fixing your weekly timesheet corrections in UKG. That’s when I realized how emotional leave conversations really are.

After the MA at our Midtown clinic self‑disclosed opioid misuse to me and Elena in June 2022—right after that near‑error with the patient’s meds—I went hunting for guidance and landed in the National Council for Mental Wellbeing micro‑course on substance‑use‑related performance issues in August 2022. It walked through last‑chance agreements and EAP referrals in a way our policies never did.

Then your ex showed up twice outside your East Sac middle school, and Mr. Alvarez cornered me about what HR should do. Cue the 3‑day Workplace Violence Prevention Institute training on domestic violence spillover in October 2023.

And of course Julie’s depressive episode and brief psych stay at Sutter Med Center last September pushed me into that 6‑week Job Accommodation Network course on serious mental‑health conditions.

So yeah, it’s a lot. But it also makes me feel less helpless when it’s our people in the middle of it.

Love,
D

metadata.subject: Why I keep signing up for every heavy HR training

artifacts.content:
Event: Weekly check‑in – Riverbend cases + family admin
Date: 2024-08-14 (repeats weekly, Wednesdays)
Time: 8:15 p.m. – 9:30 p.m.
Location: 1842 Cypress Willow Lane – kitchen table
Attendees: Daniel (solo)

Notes:

1) Riverbend clinical/ER cases to review
- Revisit MA opioid near‑miss from June 2022 at Midtown clinic; compare our current last‑chance agreement language to what the National Council for Mental Wellbeing micro‑course recommended (August 2022 module). Make sure Elena is documenting performance vs. treatment referrals separately.
- Update just‑culture coaching templates Miguel and I built this spring after Julie’s medication error during her UC Davis Med Center clinical rotation. Confirm we’re actually using them for near‑misses, not just filing them.
- Double‑check March 2023 offboarding file for the West Sacramento warehouse supervisor terminated for violating the substance‑free workplace policy. Confirm severance, COBRA packet, and “dates/title only” reference notes are all in the digital folder.
- List any open CFRA/FMLA cases that feel like Emily’s mom’s Mercy General situation (July–Oct 2022): partial shifts, messy timesheets, emotional employees.

2) Family health/finance admin
- Mom: track Kaiser South Sac schedule (chemo Saturdays, post‑chemo Sunday follow‑ups, dialysis 2nd/4th Thursdays). Make sure I’ve blocked calendar for PT at Sutter Midtown and hypertension checks.
- Dad: confirm next Elk Grove DMV ignition‑interlock calibration date and VA PTSD group at Mather.
- Julie: note next UC Davis Behavioral Health follow‑up with Dr. Patel after her medication‑related suicide attempt.

3) Personal
- Check Golden 1 repayment progress; decide whether I need extra Uber runs this week.
- Plan jog on American River Parkway at least twice before Sunday.

metadata.title: Weekly check‑in – Riverbend cases + family admin

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.t.nguyen.hr@gmail.com
To: mark.williams.hrmentor@gmail.com
Subject: Stretching into the tougher ER stuff

Hi Mark,

Wanted to give you a fuller picture of the employee‑relations work I’ve been handling since we last talked about my move into a true HR Generalist role.

Between November 2021 and February 2022 I coordinated several background check adjudications at Riverbend’s corporate office, including one memorable case where a medical billing specialist candidate had a 2019 misdemeanor theft at Arden Fair Mall. I pulled the report, talked through the context with her, drafted a risk assessment for our VP of Ops, Lisa Chen, and recommended a conditional hire with a 90‑day review. She cleared it without issues.

In March 2023, Karen Lopez asked me to quietly manage the offboarding for a long‑tenured warehouse supervisor at our West Sacramento facility who violated the substance‑free workplace policy. I prepped the severance agreement, pulled the January 2023 final written warning, coordinated COBRA, and drafted reference language restricted to dates and title. It was the first time I really felt the weight of balancing compassion with risk.

On the clinical side, Spring 2024 was big. After my sister Julie had a medication error during her UC Davis Med Center rotation, my manager Miguel and I built a just‑culture‑style coaching and documentation process for our Midtown clinic nurses. I wrote the initial talking points and forms we now use for near‑misses.

Outside of Riverbend, I’ve been informally “HR” for my uncle Thomas’s Vietnamese restaurant on Stockton Blvd—uncovering buddy‑punching that cost him about $1,200 in overpaid wages, creating simple onboarding checklists, a tip log, and corrective action forms.

All of this, plus finally passing SHRM‑CP in June 2024, makes me feel ready to own a Generalist desk, salary‑wise hopefully in the $58k+ range.

Would love your thoughts on how to frame these stories on my résumé and in interviews.

Best,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Stretching into the tougher ER stuff

artifacts.content:
Title: Big picture – caring for Mom, Dad, Julie, and still having a career

I keep telling recruiters I want a “standard” Monday–Friday 8–5:30 schedule, but the truth is my life is anything but standard.

Mom: between diabetes, neuropathy, the mild stroke in March 2023, and now chemo at Kaiser South Sacramento, my weeks revolve around her appointments. Mondays 7 p.m. PT at Kaiser South Sac, every other Friday morning hypertension checks with the $40 copay, 2nd/4th Thursday dialysis with Dr. Huynh, Saturday chemo blocks that run 8–1 plus a few hours at her Elk Grove apartment, and Sunday post‑chemo follow‑ups through at least March 2026. I’m the default driver.

Dad: back surgery at Mercy General in Jan 2023 means stacks of SSDI paperwork and medical bills we spread across the dining table in Elk Grove most Monday nights. On top of that, there’s the monthly ignition‑interlock calibration at Elk Grove DMV from his second DUI in October 2023 and the PTSD group at the Sacramento VA on first Mondays.

Julie: after the depressive episode and brief hospitalization at Sutter Medical Center last September, I’ve been taking her to UC Davis Behavioral Health follow‑ups with Dr. Patel on the last Friday of each month, and starting October 2024 I promised to pick her up after UC Davis Med Center clinicals so she doesn’t have to ride light rail alone after the harassment incident in May.

Me: CBT Fridays with Dr. Howard at Sutter Mental Health, the HR burnout group at Sacramento Community Wellness Center on Saturdays, and the Vietnamese caregivers’ support group at UC Davis Med Center on Sundays for Dad’s early dementia and Mom’s finances.

Somehow in the middle of all this, I’m still the “reliable HR guy” at Riverbend, dreaming about a Generalist role and a calm life with Emily.

metadata.title: Big picture – caring for Mom, Dad, Julie, and still having a career
